meta.title: E88 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series E88 engine — N43, N46, N52, N54, N55, N47. Real UK costs, failure data, and honest reliability advice. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series E88 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E88 engine typically costs £1,800–£6,500 supply only, depending on engine — the four-cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,800, while the N54 twin-turbo can reach £3,500–£6,500. Fitting adds £520–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series E88 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 3.0-litre naturally aspirated six-cylinder is the most reliable E88 engine — smooth, linear power, no turbo complexity, and no HPFP worries. The N55 single-turbo is also a reliable performance option, while the N47 diesel requires careful timing chain history.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series E88?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N47 diesel, HPFP and injector failure on the N54 twin-turbo, water pump failure on the N52, and VANOS solenoid issues on all six-cylinder variants — plus the convertible-specific roof mechanism problems common to the E88.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series E88 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability varies significantly by engine choice. The N52 six-cylinder is the most dependable, while the N54 is the most complex and expensive to maintain. The N47 diesel has a well-documented timing chain risk but is otherwise robust. A well-maintained E88 can be a rewarding car; a neglected one will be expensive.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series E88 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N54 twin-turbo without documented HPFP and injector history — it's a high-performance engine with high-performance maintenance costs. A pre-2010 N47 diesel without timing chain replacement history is also a risky purchase. No engine is inherently bad, but history is everything.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E88 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle (N47 timing chain), long cranking or limp mode (N54 HPFP), overheating with no obvious external leak (N52 water pump), or blue smoke and rough idle (any high-mileage engine) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series E88 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000 E88, a £4,000 N47 recon is 50% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 100% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line. On a well-kept example, replacement makes sense; on a cheap one, it doesn't.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 1 Series E88 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 six-cylinder can exceed 200,000 miles with proper maintenance. The N47 diesel's limiting factor is the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can also reach high mileage. The N54 and N55 turbos are durable but require meticulous maintenance, particularly for the HPFP and injectors.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E88 and the E82?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E88 is the convertible variant of the first-generation 1 Series (2007–2013), with a folding fabric roof and additional chassis bracing for structural rigidity. The E82 is the coupé variant — a fixed-roof 2+2 with slightly sharper handling dynamics and lower weight. Both share the same engine range and platform.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series E88 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on N47/N52/N54/N55 units. Tell us your variant (118i, 120d, 135i, etc.) and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 1 Series E88 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E88 convertible — from the N47 four-cylinder diesel to the N54 twin-turbo six. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the N47 timing chain risk that defines this generation.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series E88 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E88 convertible — from the N43 four-cylinder petrol to the N54 twin-turbo six and the N47 turbodiesel.

overview.intro: The E88 is the convertible variant of BMW's first-generation 1 Series, launched in 2007 as the E82 coupé's drop-top sibling. Built alongside the E81/E82/E87, the E88 shares the same rear-wheel-drive platform, 50:50 weight balance, and engine range — including the N47 diesel and the turbocharged N54/N55 six-cylinder. Production ran until 2013.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E8x (shared with E81, E82, E87) • Predecessor: None — first 1 Series convertible • Successor: BMW 2 Series Convertible (F23) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E81 (3-door hatch), E82 (coupé), E87 (5-door hatch)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a lower-volume convertible variant, the E88 is a niche enthusiast vehicle — the 135i remains the most sought-after, while the 120d is the most practical daily driver.

items.quote: The E88's engine range spans three distinct characters. The N52 3.0-litre naturally aspirated six is the most dependable petrol option — smooth, linear power, and none of the turbo-related complexity that defines the N54. The N55 3.0-litre single-turbo (2010–2013) is a significant improvement over the earlier N54, with fewer HPFP failures and more reliable wastegate design. The N47 2.0-litre diesel is the most economical choice but carries this generation's defining risk: the rear-mounted timing chain can stretch and fail catastrophically. If you're buying an E88, the N52 six is the sensible choice; the N55 is the performance sweet spot; the N47 requires documented chain history.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — reliable, smooth, no turbo worries
High Risk — fast but expensive to maintain
Best Turbo — N54's successor, more reliable
Risk vs Reward — chain history essential

economicsRule.title: The E88 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,000 N47 recon against a £5,000 E88 sits at 50–100% of the car's value — right at our 70% BMW threshold. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 31–63% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Against a sub-£4,000 E88, a major engine failure is a write-off. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E88 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N52 3.0 — no turbo, simpler than N54/N55.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E88 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N54 twin-turbo — HPFP and turbo repairs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E88 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: N52 125i/128i — six-cylinder character without the turbo bills.

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N47's timing chain is mounted at the rear of the engine, making it a major labour-intensive job to replace. The chain, guides, and tensioners can wear prematurely, causing chain stretch. If the chain stretches too far, it can "jump" a tooth or snap, causing pistons to strike open valves. Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on an N47 diesel E88, get the timing chain inspected immediately. A £1,000–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E88 worth under £5,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your E88 →

problems.title: N54 HPFP & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The high-pressure fuel pump on the N54 is a known weak point — internal wear leads to fuel pressure loss, hard starting, and eventual engine damage. Injector issues frequently follow, with the injectors failing to seal properly and causing fuel to wash the cylinder walls.
problems.recommendation: If your E88 135i has no documented HPFP history, treat it as overdue preventative maintenance. £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows a lean-running failure.

cta.label: Compare N54 engine prices for your E88 135i →

problems.title: N52 Water Pump & VANOS Solenoids
problems.rootCause: The N52's electric water pump has a finite service life and can fail without warning, leading to overheating. The VANOS solenoids can also become clogged or fail, causing rough idle, reduced power, and check engine lights.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 80,000 miles on any N52-powered E88. A £400–£800 preventative job is far cheaper than a £2,500+ engine replacement. Clean the VANOS solenoids at the same time.

cta.label: Compare N52 engine prices for your E88 →
